definition: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability: classical transaction properties.
Set of objects treated as one unit for reads, writes, distribution and often atomic updates.
Prefers availability over consistency during a network partition.
Basically Available, Soft State, Eventual Consistency.
During a network partition, consistency and availability cannot both be fully guaranteed.
All nodes agree on the order of operations that may be causally related.
Prefers consistency over availability during a network partition.
If no further updates occur, all replicas eventually converge to a consistent state.
Partial event order induced by local order, send-before-receive and transitivity.
Scalar logical clock using max(local, received)+1 on receive; satisfies the weak clock property.
After a session has read a version, it will not later read an older version.
If partitioned, availability versus consistency; else latency versus consistency.
Read and write quorums with 2W>N and R+W>N ensure overlaps.
After writing, a session reads at least that version or a newer one.
Storage of multiple copies of the same data on different nodes.
Read One, Write All: R=1 and W=N.
Distribution of different data partitions across nodes; horizontal fragmentation.
Vector of logical counters that captures causality exactly and makes concurrent events incomparable.
Log-structured hash table with append-only data files and the full keydir in memory.
Probabilistic membership structure with no false negatives but possible false positives.
Merging and reorganizing files to remove old versions and tombstones.
Sparse index entry storing, for example, a block's smallest key and its location.
Conversion of an immutable memtable into a new SSTable on level 0.
Bitcask helper file containing value positions and sizes for faster keydir reconstruction.
Bitcask's in-memory hash table mapping keys to file, offset, size and timestamp of the newest version.
LSM compaction with non-overlapping SSTables in higher levels; read-friendly.
Write-optimized structure of sorted memtables and hierarchically organized SSTables.
Append-only history of SSTables added to and removed from an LSM store.
Sorted in-memory structure for the newest LSM writes.
Additional physical reads needed to answer one logical read.
Immutable file of key-value pairs sorted by key.
LSM compaction that allows overlapping SSTables on a level; write-friendly.
Special value marking a logical deletion in append-only structures.
Persistent log that protects changes before they are reflected in volatile main structures.
Physical amount of data written relative to the logical amount of data written.
MongoDB replica-set member with a vote but without a data copy.
Binary JSON-like representation used internally by MongoDB.
Intentional redundancy to avoid joins or additional reads.
Database that understands structured document values and can query their fields.
Storing a dependent object as a nested subdocument inside the owning document.
MongoDB query router that forwards client requests to the responsible shards.
MongoDB setting describing the required visibility or confirmation level of read data.
MongoDB setting describing which replica-set member should serve reads.
MongoDB replication group with a primary, secondaries and optionally an arbiter.
Field or field combination used by MongoDB to distribute documents of a collection.
MongoDB setting describing how many and which durability acknowledgements a write requires.
Cypher function returning all equally short shortest paths for a match.
Declarative, pattern-based query language for property graphs.
Deletes a node together with all incoming and outgoing relationships.
Direct storage of a node's neighborhood, similar to pointers to adjacent nodes.
Tag describing the role or class of a node in a property graph.
Cypher clause that matches the complete pattern first and creates it only if no match exists.
Constraint combining existence and uniqueness of a property combination for a label.
Graph model with labeled nodes, named directed relationships and properties on both.
Within one Cypher pattern, the same stored relationship cannot be matched twice.
Cypher function returning one shortest path per match.
Cypher clause that passes intermediate results to the next query part.
Stores one bit string over all row positions for each possible column value.
Explicit group of related wide-column columns and unit of access control.
System that physically stores relational values by column rather than by row.
Replaces values with short identifiers into a separate dictionary.
Early reconstruction of complete tuples from separately read columns.
Keeps columns separate as long as possible and reconstructs values only after filters and joins.
Compression of sparse columns by omitting their many NULL values.
Stores consecutive equal values as a pair of value and repetition count.
Single Instruction, Multiple Data: one CPU instruction processes several column values in parallel.
Bigtable group of consecutive rows and unit of distribution and load balancing.
Implicit tuple identifier derived from a value's position in its column.
Sparse map that typically addresses values by row key, column and timestamp.
